An airplane in flight is subject to an air resistance force proportional to the square of its speed \( v \). But there is an additional resistive force because the airplane has wings. Air flowing over the wings is pushed down and slightly forward, so from Newton's third law the air exerts a force on the wings and airplane that is up and slightly backward as shown in figure. The upward force is the lift force that keeps the airplane aloft, and the backward force is called induced drag. At flying speeds, induced drag is inversely proportional to \( v^2 \), so the total air resistance force can be expressed by \( F_{air} = \alpha v^2 + \beta/v^2 \), where \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are positive constants that depend on the shape and size of the airplane and the density of the air. For a Cessna 150, a small single-engine airplane, \(\alpha = 0.30 \, \mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{s}^2/\mathrm{m}^2\) and \(\beta = 3.5 \times 10^5 \, \mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{m}^2/\mathrm{s}^2\). In steady flight, the engine must provide a forward force that exactly balances the air resistance force. Calculate the speed (in km/h) for which the airplane will have the maximum endurance (that is, remain in the air the longest time).
after_value: An airplane in flight is subject to an air resistance force proportional to the square of its speed \( v \). But there is an additional resistive force because the airplane has wings. Air flowing over the wings is pushed down and slightly forward, so from Newton's third law the air exerts a force on the wings and airplane that is up and slightly backward as shown in the figure. The upward force is the lift force that keeps the airplane aloft, and the backward force is called induced drag. At flying speeds, induced drag is inversely proportional to \( v^2 \), so the total air resistance force can be expressed by \( F_{air} = \alpha v^2 + \beta / v^2 \), where \( \alpha \) and \( \beta \) are positive constants that depend on the shape and size of the airplane and the density of the air. For a Cessna 150, a small single-engine airplane, \( \alpha = 0.30 \, \mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{s}^2 / \mathrm{m}^2 \) and \( \beta = 3.5 \times 10^5 \, \mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{m}^2 / \mathrm{s}^2 \). In steady flight, the engine must provide a forward force that exactly balances the air resistance force. Calculate the speed (in km/h) for which the airplane will have the maximum endurance (that is, remain in the air the longest time).

before_value: 90km/h

before_value: Many of the stars in the sky are actually binary stars, in which two stars orbit about their common center of mass. If the orbital speeds of the stars are high enough, the motion of the stars can be detected by the Doppler shifts of the light they emit. Stars for which this is the case are called \emph{spectroscopic binary stars}. Figure shows the simplest case of a spectroscopic binary star: two identical stars orbiting their center of mass in a circle. The plane of the stars' orbits is edge-on to the line of sight of an observer on the earth. The light from each star in the binary system varies from its maximum frequency to its minimum frequency and back again in 11.0 days. Determine the orbital radius \( R \) and the mass \( m \) of each star. Give your answer for \( m \) in kilograms and as a multiple of the mass of the sun, \( 1.99 \times 10^{30} \) kg. Compare the value of \( R \) to the distance from the earth to the sun, \( 1.50 \times 10^{11} \) m.

before_value: 365 nm

A helium--neon laser can produce a green laser beam instead of a red one. Figure shows the transitions involved to form the red beam and the green beam. After a population inversion is established, neon atoms make a variety of downward transitions in falling from the state labeled \( E_4^* \) down eventually to level \( E_1 \) (arbitrarily assigned the energy \( E_1 = 0 \)). The atoms emit both red light with a wavelength of 632.8 nm in a transition \( E_4^* - E_3 \) and green light with a wavelength of 543 nm in a competing transition \( E_4^* - E_2 \). What is the energy \( E_2 \)? Assume the atoms are in a cavity between mirrors designed to reflect the green light with high efficiency but to allow the red light to leave the cavity immediately. Then stimulated emission can lead to the buildup of a collimated beam of green light between the mirrors having a greater intensity than that of the red light. To constitute the radiated laser beam, a small fraction of the green light is permitted to escape by transmission through one mirror. The mirrors forming the resonant cavity can be made of layers of silicon dioxide (index of refraction \( n = 1.458 \)) and titanium dioxide (index of refraction varies between 1.9 and 2.6).
after_value: A helium--neon laser can produce a green laser beam instead of a red one. The figure shows the transitions involved to form the red beam and the green beam. What is the energy \(E_2\)? Assume the atoms are in a cavity between mirrors designed to reflect the green light with high efficiency but to allow the red light to leave the cavity immediately. Then stimulated emission can lead to the buildup of a collimated beam of green light between the mirrors having a greater intensity than that of the red light. To constitute the radiated laser beam, a small fraction of the green light is permitted to escape by transmission through one mirror. The mirrors forming the resonant cavity can be made of layers of silicon dioxide (index of refraction \(n = 1.458\)) and titanium dioxide (index of refraction varies between 1.9 and 2.6).

The identical bunches of molecules thus formed struck and adhered to a curved glass plate fixed to the interior drum wall, the fastest molecules striking near A, which was opposite \(\text{S}_2\), the slowest near B, and the others in between depending on their speeds. The density (proportional to the number of molecules) plotted against distance along the glass plate (dependent on \( v \)) made possible determination of the speed distribution. The circuit shown in Figure forms a relaxation oscillator. It is composed of a neon bulb and a capacitor. The capacitor is charged by a DC power source through a resistor. The neon bulb quickly returns to infinite resistance. Find the flashing frequency f of the neon bulb.

A Fabry-Perot interferometer is composed of a pair of very smooth, parallel planar surfaces. The surfaces are coated with a film to reflect and transmit light. For light intensity incident perpendicularly on the film, the reflected portion is $R^2$, and the transmitted portion is $T^2$.As shown in Figure, light with intensity $I_0$ and wavelength $\lambda$ is incident from the left onto one of the surfaces. Part of the light is directly transmitted through the system, while other light is reflected by the second surface, then reflected by the first surface, and then transmitted out from the second surface. In general, the light beam transmitted out of the system is synthesized from light that has undergone 0, 2, 4, 6,... internal reflections before being transmitted out from the second surface. How does the relative transmitted intensity $I_t / I_0$ depend on $D, \lambda, R,$ and $T$?
after_value: A Fabry-Perot interferometer is composed of a pair of very smooth, parallel planar surfaces. The surfaces are coated with a film to reflect and transmit light. For light intensity incident perpendicularly on the film, the reflected portion is $R^2$, and the transmitted portion is $T^2$. As shown in the figure, light with intensity $I_0$ and wavelength $\lambda$ is incident from the left onto one of the surfaces. Part of the light is directly transmitted through the system, while other light is reflected by the second surface, then reflected by the first surface, and then transmitted out from the second surface. In general, the light beam transmitted out of the system is synthesized from light that has undergone 0, 2, 4, 6, ... internal reflections before being transmitted out from the second surface. How does the relative transmitted intensity $I_t / I_0$ depend on $D, \lambda, R,$ and $T$?

before_value: \( \frac{T^4}{2R^2(1-\cos(4\pi D/\lambda)) + T^4} \)

The results from a common type of grating spectrometer are shown in Figure. Light emitted from light source L passes through slit S, and then passes through a collimating lens (or mirror) C₁ to become parallel light (such that the light incident on the grating is like a plane wave coming from infinity). Then, this parallel light is diffracted by grating G. The diffracted light (propagating forward within a certain angular range) is incident on another lens C₂ and is focused onto plane P. On plane P, the spectrum appears as bands, and at different positions, narrow spectral lines are found. Let the length and width of the slit be $h$ and $w$ respectively, the focal lengths of C₁ and C₂ be $F_1$ and $F_2$ respectively, the angles between the grating normal and the optical axes of C₁ and C₂ be $\theta_i$ and $\theta_d$, and the number of lines per millimeter on the grating be $N$. If the slit width $w$ is much greater than the resolution of the collimating lens $1.22\lambda F_1 / A_1$, where $A_1$ is the aperture of the collimating lens, then what is the width $w'$ of the spectral line on plane P?
after_value: The results from a common type of grating spectrometer are shown in Figure. Light emitted from light source L passes through slit S, and then passes through a collimating lens (or mirror) C1 to become parallel light (such that the light incident on the grating is like a plane wave coming from infinity). Then, this parallel light is diffracted by grating G. The diffracted light (propagating forward within a certain angular range) is incident on another lens C2 and is focused onto plane P. On plane P, the spectrum appears as bands, and at different positions, narrow spectral lines are found. Let the length and width of the slit be h and w respectively, the focal lengths of C1 and C2 be F_1 and F_2 respectively, the angles between the grating normal and the optical axes of C1 and C2 be \theta_i and \theta_d, and the number of lines per millimeter on the grating be N. If the slit width w is much greater than the resolution of the collimating lens 1.22\lambda F_1 / A_1, where A_1 is the aperture of the collimating lens, then what is the width w' of the spectral line on plane P?

before_value: \( \omega \frac{F_2}{F_1} \frac{\cos \theta_i}{\cos \theta_d} \)

The focal length of the instrument is $F = 23 \text{ m}$, the grating's ruled area is 15 cm × 25 cm, and it has 600 lines per millimeter. What value should the tilt angle $\theta$ of the grating take so that the 5th order spectral line of neutral iron at $\lambda = 5250.218 \text{ Å}$ coincides with the entrance slit?

before_value: As shown in Figure, an electron initially at rest at $x = 0, z = 0$ is subjected to an acceleration $\boldsymbol{a}$ for $t > 0$. $\boldsymbol{a}$ is in the $xz$-plane and makes an angle $\theta$ with the $z$-axis. Calculate (relativistically) the electric field $\boldsymbol{E}(t)$ at the point $(0, 0, R_0)$, where $R_0$ is a 'large' distance. Express the result in terms of $\boldsymbol{a}$, $\theta$, and $\beta = v/c$, where $v$ is the electron's speed.
after_value: As shown in Figure, an electron initially at rest at $x = 0, z = 0$ is subjected to an acceleration $\boldsymbol{a}$ for $t > 0$. $\boldsymbol{a}$ is in the $xz$-plane and makes an angle $\theta$ with the $z$-axis. before_value: \( \frac{a \sin \theta}{(1 - \beta \cos \theta)^3} \boldsymbol{e}_x \)

Figure shows a single-gap spark chamber. The gap between the two aluminum plates of the spark chamber is $d$. A charged cosmic ray particle ionizes neon molecules along its path. After time $t$, a high-voltage pulse is applied across the two aluminum plates, and sparks are observed along the particle's path. In order to eliminate residual electrons and ions in the spark chamber from cosmic rays that entered previously, a DC voltage is applied across the two aluminum plates. This "clearing voltage" similarly sweeps away the electrons and ions we wish to observe. When the pulse is applied, what voltage $V_D$ will completely clear the space of electrons?
after_value: Figure shows a single-gap spark chamber. The gap between the two aluminum plates of the spark chamber is $d$. A charged cosmic ray particle ionizes neon molecules along its path. before_value: An ideal gas sample with $\gamma = 4/3$ changes from state A (pressure 1 atm, volume 22.4 L, temperature 300 K) to state C (pressure 2 atm, volume 33.6 L, temperature 900 K) via two paths—ABC and ADC, as shown in Figure. Calculate $\Delta S$.
after_value: An ideal gas sample with $\gamma = 4/3$ changes from state A (pressure 1 atm, volume 22.4 L, temperature 300 K) to state C (pressure 2 atm, volume 33.6 L, temperature 900 K) via two paths—ABC and ADC, as shown in the figure. Calculate $\Delta S$.

before_value: Two "identical" objects with constant heat capacity (as $C_p$), with initial temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ respectively, are used as heat sources for a Carnot engine (operating in an infinitesimally small reversible cycle), as shown in Figure. If the pressure of the two objects remains constant and no phase change occurs.Find the total work $W$ done by the heat engine.
after_value: Two "identical" objects with constant heat capacity (as $C_p$), with initial temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ respectively, are used as heat sources for a Carnot engine (operating in an infinitesimally small reversible cycle), as shown in the figure. If the pressure of the two objects remains constant and no phase change occurs, find the total work $W$ done by the heat engine.

before_value: The ideal turbine engine cycle includes adiabatic compression from A to B, isobaric heat absorption process from B to C, adiabatic expansion from C to D, and isobaric heat release process from D to A; all stages are reversible as shown in figure. Assume the working fluid in the heat engine is an ideal gas, and the ratio of its specific heat at constant pressure to its specific heat at constant volume is $\gamma$. The gas flow rate through the heat engine per unit time is $\dot{N}$ moles. Furthermore, assume the highest temperature $T_C$ of the gas is determined by the maximum temperature the high-temperature part of the heat engine can withstand, and the lowest temperature $T_A$ of the gas is determined by the ambient air temperature. If the power generated by the heat engine is 1 MW, and the highest temperature is $T_C = 1200$ K, then under ambient conditions ($P_A = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa, $T_A = 300$ K), what is the volume flow rate $\dot{V}_A$ of air ($\gamma=7/5$) in the heat engine?
after_value: The ideal turbine engine cycle includes adiabatic compression from A to B, isobaric heat absorption from B to C, adiabatic expansion from C to D, and isobaric heat release from D to A; all stages are reversible as shown in the figure. Assume the working fluid is an ideal gas, and the ratio of its specific heat at constant pressure to its specific heat at constant volume is $\gamma$. The gas flow rate through the heat engine per unit time is $\dot{N}$ moles. The highest temperature $T_C$ of the gas is determined by the maximum temperature the high-temperature part of the heat engine can withstand, and the lowest temperature $T_A$ of the gas is determined by the ambient air temperature. If the power generated by the heat engine is 1 MW, and the highest temperature is $T_C = 1200\,\mathrm{K}$, then under ambient conditions ($P_A = 1.013 \times 10^5\,\mathrm{Pa}$, $T_A = 300\,\mathrm{K}$), what is the volume flow rate $\dot{V}_A$ of air in the heat engine? Assume $\gamma = 7/5$.

before_value: \( 2.8 \mathrm{ m}^3 \mathrm{ s}^{-1} \)

Two volumes are separated by a stopcock. At time $t=0$, the stopcock is opened. $V_2 = 2V_1$ as shown in figure. After a long time, for the following case $V_1$ contains 1 mole of nitrogen gas, and $V_2$ contains 2 moles of nitrogen gas. Calculate the change in entropy of the gas, $\Delta S$.
after_value: Two volumes are separated by a stopcock. At time $t=0$, the stopcock is opened. $V_2 = 2V_1$ as shown in the figure. After a long time, for the following case $V_1$ contains 1 mole of nitrogen gas, and $V_2$ contains 2 moles of nitrogen gas. Calculate the change in entropy of the gas, $\Delta S$.

before_value: As shown in Figure, six sliders, each of mass $m$, are connected by identical springs and are confined to move within a linear air track. How many independent modes of vibration are there?
after_value: As shown in Figure, six sliders, each of mass $m$, are connected by identical springs and are confined to move within a linear air track. How many independent modes of vibration are there?

before_value: The construction of a simple musical instrument is as follows: A string of negligible mass is tightly stretched between two fixed points. A mass is attached at a distance from one end, as shown in Figure. The object is pulled away from its equilibrium position by a small distance A ($A \ll x, A \ll l-x$), and then vibrates. Calculate the frequency of the sound wave, ν.
after_value: The construction of a simple musical instrument is as follows: A string of negligible mass is tightly stretched between two fixed points. A mass is attached at a distance from one end, as shown in Figure. The object is pulled away from its equilibrium position by a small distance A ($A \ll x, A \ll l-x$), and then vibrates. Calculate the frequency of the sound wave, ν.

before_value: \( \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{T}{m} \frac{l}{x(l-x)}} \)

Two identical cylinders, each with moment of inertia $I$, are suspended by identical steel wires with torsional constant $K$, as shown in Figure. No swinging is allowed. How many modes of vibration are there?
after_value: Two identical cylinders, each with moment of inertia $I$, are suspended by identical steel wires with torsional constant $K$, as shown in the figure. No swinging is allowed. How many modes of vibration are there?

before_value: Two sound sources S₁ and S₂ are separated, as shown in figure. There are two observers at points A and B, which are on the same line as the sound sources. S₁ emits a stable plane wave, whose excess pressure is given by $p_e = A \cos(200t)$; the excess pressure of the stable plane wave emitted by S₂ is given by $p_e = A \cos(210t)$. The speed of sound is taken as 340 m s⁻¹. What is the time interval $\Delta t$ between the maximum pulses heard by A and B?
after_value: Two sound sources S1 and S2 are separated, as shown in the figure. There are two observers at points A and B, which are on the same line as the sound sources. S1 emits a stable plane wave, whose excess pressure is given by $p_e = A \cos(200t)$; the excess pressure of the stable plane wave emitted by S2 is given by $p_e = A \cos(210t)$. The speed of sound is taken as 340 m s⁻¹. What is the time interval $\Delta t$ between the maximum pulses heard by A and B?

before_value: Two identical objects are hung on two identical springs as shown in Figure. No swinging is allowed. How many normal modes. are there?
after_value: Two identical objects are hung on two identical springs as shown in the figure. No swinging is allowed. How many normal modes are there?

before_value: As shown in Figure, a guitar string is pulled up from the middle of its length. Calculate the amplitudes of the third harmonics, A₃ (relative to the amplitude of the fundamental mode A₀).
after_value: As shown in Figure, a guitar string is pulled up from the middle of its length. Calculate the amplitudes of the third harmonics, A3 (relative to the amplitude of the fundamental mode A0).

before_value: A full-wave rectifier is an instrument that can convert a sine wave with amplitude V₀ into the waveform shown in Figure. Find the amplitude A₂ of the second harmonic component of the output voltage.
after_value: A full-wave rectifier is an instrument that can convert a sine wave with amplitude V0 into the waveform shown in Figure. Find the amplitude A2 of the second harmonic component of the output voltage.

before_value: In a certain region of space, there is a uniform electric field E with magnitude 10,000 V cm⁻¹ along the positive x-axis. In the same region, there is also a uniform magnetic field B along the y-axis. A beam of muons with velocity $v = c/3$ passes straight through this region along the positive z-axis, as shown in Figure. It is known that the mass of a muon is 207 times that of an electron, and its charge magnitude is the same as that of an electron. Find the magnitude of the magnetic field.
after_value: In a certain region of space, there is a uniform electric field E with magnitude 10,000 V cm⁻¹ along the positive x-axis. In the same region, there is also a uniform magnetic field B along the y-axis. A beam of muons with velocity $v = c/3$ passes straight through this region along the positive z-axis, as shown in Figure. It is known that the mass of a muon is 207 times that of an electron, and its charge magnitude is the same as that of an electron. Find the magnitude of the magnetic field.

before_value: Refer to Figure. There is a line charge of length $(l_1 + l_2)\text{m}$ with charge density $\lambda \text{ C/m}$. The distance from point $P$ to the line is $r$. Find the electric potential $\phi$ at point $P$.
after_value: Refer to Figure. There is a line charge of length $(l_1 + l_2)\text{ m}$ with charge density $\lambda\text{ C/m}$. The distance from point $P$ to the line is $r$. Find the electric potential $\phi$ at point $P$.

before_value: \[ \phi(P) = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \ln \left[ \frac{l_2 + \sqrt{r^2 + l_2^2}}{-l_1 + \sqrt{r^2 + l_1^2}} \right] \]
As shown in Figure, a thin circular disk has a uniform surface charge density σ. Point P is on the axis of the disk, at a distance from the center of the disk Find the electric field E at point P.
after_value: As shown in Figure, a thin circular disk has a uniform surface charge density σ. Point P is on the axis of the disk, at a distance from the center of the disk. Find the electric field E at point P.

before_value: \[ \left( \frac{\lambda}{2\pi \epsilon_0 r} \right) \boldsymbol{e}_r \]

Two large metal plates are placed parallel to each other. One end of the two plates is connected by a metallic strip. A thin plastic sheet with a surface charge density $\sigma$ is placed between the two plates. As shown in Figure. Calculate the electric field $\vec{E}_1$ near the upper plate.
after_value: Two large metal plates are placed parallel to each other. One end of the two plates is connected by a metallic strip. A thin plastic sheet with a surface charge density $\sigma$ is placed between the two plates as shown in the figure. Calculate the electric field $\vec{E}_1$ near the upper plate.

before_value: \[ \left( \frac{2\sigma}{3\epsilon_0} \right) e_z \]
As shown in Figure, there are two concentric metal spheres. The inner sphere has a charge $q'$, and the outer sphere has a charge $q$. Find the potential $\phi$ on the surface of the inner sphere.
after_value: As shown in Figure, there are two concentric metal spheres. The inner sphere has a charge $q'$, and the outer sphere has a charge $q$. Find the potential $\phi$ on the surface of the inner sphere.

before_value: D
As shown in Figure, there is a charge $+q$ at a distance from an uncharged conducting sphere. Find the electric potential $\phi$ of the sphere.
after_value: As shown in Figure, there is a charge $+q$ at a distance from an uncharged conducting sphere. Find the electric potential $\phi$ of the sphere.

before_value: \[ \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \frac{q}{b} \]

In an electron tube, electrons are emitted from a hot metal plate (emitter) and collected by a metal plate (collector) parallel to the emitter. The two metal plates are separated by a distance $d$. As shown in Figure. (Compared to the lateral dimensions of the metal plates, the distance $d$ is very small.) The potential between the two plates is given by $\phi = kx^{4/3}$, where $x$ is the distance from the emitter. For $0 < x < d$, what is the volume charge density $\rho(x)$?
after_value: In an electron tube, electrons are emitted from a hot metal plate (emitter) and collected by a metal plate (collector) parallel to the emitter. The two metal plates are separated by a distance $d$. As shown in Figure. (Compared to the lateral dimensions of the metal plates, the distance $d$ is very small.) The potential between the two plates is given by $\phi = kx^{4/3}$, where $x$ is the distance from the emitter. For $0 < x < d$, what is the volume charge density $\rho(x)$?

before_value: As shown in Figure, a charge $+q$ is uniformly distributed on a thin circular ring. The ring is located in the $yz$ plane with its center at the origin, and a charge $-q$ is placed at the center of the ring. Find the potential $\phi$ at point $P=(x, 0, 0)$.
after_value: As shown in Figure, a charge $+q$ is uniformly distributed on a thin circular ring. The ring is located in the $yz$ plane with its center at the origin, and a charge $-q$ is placed at the center of the ring. Find the potential $\phi$ at point $P=(x, 0, 0)$.

before_value: \[ \frac{q}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \left[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{x^2 + a^2}} - \frac{1}{x} \right] \]

A parallel-plate capacitor has a capacitance of 100 pF (1 pF = 10⁻¹² F). The separation between the plates is 1 cm. It is charged by a battery to a potential difference of 10 V. Then the capacitor is disconnected. The lower plate is illuminated with blue light, causing electrons with kinetic energy from 0 to 1.5 eV to be emitted. The potential difference attracts these electrons to the upper plate. The total current as a function of time is shown in Figure. After what time (t₁ in the figure) does the potential difference between the two plates become zero?
after_value: A parallel-plate capacitor has a capacitance of 100 pF (1 pF = 10^-12 F). The separation between the plates is 1 cm. It is charged by a battery to a potential difference of 10 V. Then the capacitor is disconnected. The lower plate is illuminated with blue light, causing electrons with kinetic energy from 0 to 1.5 eV to be emitted. The potential difference attracts these electrons to the upper plate. The total current as a function of time is shown in the figure. After what time (t1 in the figure) does the potential difference between the two plates become zero?

before_value: The plates of a parallel-plate capacitor are squares with side length 20 cm. The separation between the plates is 1 cm. The capacitor is charged to a potential difference of 10V and then disconnected from the battery. As shown in Figure, a large square dielectric plate with thickness slightly less than 1 cm is inserted between the two plates, so that an area of 10 cm × 20 cm within the capacitor is filled by the dielectric. The dielectric constant of the dielectric is 4.0. At positions inside the capacitor (thus edge effects can be neglected), what is the dipole moment per unit volume, P, of the dielectric?
after_value: The plates of a parallel-plate capacitor are squares with side length 20 cm. The separation between the plates is 1 cm. The capacitor is charged to a potential difference of 10 V and then disconnected from the battery. As shown in the figure, a large square dielectric plate with thickness slightly less than 1 cm is inserted between the two plates, so that an area of 10 cm × 20 cm within the capacitor is filled by the dielectric. The dielectric constant of the dielectric is 4.0. At positions inside the capacitor (thus edge effects can be neglected), what is the dipole moment per unit volume, P, of the dielectric?

before_value: Consider a dielectric sphere of radius a, which is uniformly polarized with polarization vector P, as shown in Figure. Find the electric field inside and outside the sphere.
after_value: Consider a dielectric sphere of radius a, which is uniformly polarized with polarization vector P, as shown in Figure. Find the electric field inside and outside the sphere.

before_value: \[ -\frac{P}{3\epsilon_0} \mathbf{e}_z \]

Four parallel copper wires of size 10 are arranged such that their cross-sections form a square. Each wire carries a current, as shown in Figure. What are the magnitude and direction of the force F per meter experienced by the bottom-left wire?
after_value: Four parallel copper wires of size 10 are arranged such that their cross-sections form a square. Each wire carries a current, as shown in Figure. What are the magnitude and direction of the force F per meter experienced by the bottom-left wire?

before_value: \[ (-6\boldsymbol{e}_x + 2\boldsymbol{e}_y) 10^{-4} \mathrm{N\,m^{-1}} \]

A long coaxial cable consists of two concentric conductors, as shown in Figure. The currents in the two conductors have the same magnitude I but opposite directions. It can be assumed that the current is uniformly distributed within the conductors. Find B at a radial distance r where b < r < c.
after_value: A long coaxial cable consists of two concentric conductors, as shown in Figure. The currents in the two conductors have the same magnitude I but opposite directions. It can be assumed that the current is uniformly distributed within the conductors. Find B at a radial distance r where b < r < c.

before_value: \[ \frac{I}{2\pi\epsilon_0 c^2 r} \left[ \frac{c^2 - r^2}{c^2 - b^2} \right] \]

A long straight wire carries a current $I_1$. A rectangular loop has length $l$ and width $w$, and carries a current $I_2$. The wire is in the plane of the loop and is parallel to the long sides of the loop, as shown in Figure. Find the force on the wire, $F_{\text{wire}}$.
after_value: A long straight wire carries a current $I_1$. A rectangular loop has length $l$ and width $w$, and carries a current $I_2$. The wire is in the plane of the loop and is parallel to the long sides of the loop, as shown in the figure. Find the force on the wire, $F_{\text{wire}}$.

before_value: \[ -\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{loop}} \]

Two pipes are separated by a distance d, entering perpendicularly and passing through a large wall of thickness t, as shown in Figure. The thermal conductivity of the wall is K, and the temperature far from the pipes is T₀. At x = +d/2, hot water flows in the pipe, transferring heat to the wall at a rate of W Watts. At x = -d/2, cold water flows in the pipe, absorbing heat from the wall at a rate of W Watts. Consider this as a two-dimensional problem, neglecting the finite size effects of the pipes. Find the temperature Tp at point P(x = 100d, y = 100d). Let T₀ = 20°C, d = 50 cm, K = 0.03 W cm⁻¹K, W = 200 W, t = 10 cm. Make appropriate approximations in the calculation.
after_value: Two pipes are separated by a distance d, entering perpendicularly and passing through a large wall of thickness t, as shown in the figure. The thermal conductivity of the wall is K, and the temperature far from the pipes is T0. At x = +d/2, hot water flows in the pipe, transferring heat to the wall at a rate of W watts. At x = -d/2, cold water flows in the pipe, absorbing heat from the wall at a rate of W watts. Consider this as a two-dimensional problem, neglecting the finite size effects of the pipes. Find the temperature Tp at point P(x = 100d, y = 100d). Let T0 = 20°C, d = 50 cm, K = 0.03 W cm^-1 K, W = 200 W, t = 10 cm. Make appropriate approximations in the calculation.

before_value: Two pipes are separated by a distance d, entering perpendicularly and passing through a large wall of thickness t, as shown in Figure. The thermal conductivity of the wall is K, and the temperature far from the pipes is T₀. At x = +d/2, hot water flows in the pipe, transferring heat to the wall at a rate of W Watts. At x = -d/2, cold water flows in the pipe, absorbing heat from the wall at a rate of W Watts. Consider this as a two-dimensional problem, neglecting the finite size effects of the pipes. Find the temperature Tp at point P(x = 100d, y = 100d). Let T₀ = 20°C, d = 50 cm, K = 0.03 W cm⁻¹K, W = 200 W, t = 10 cm. Make appropriate approximations in the calculation.

The scanning function of a television picture tube is achieved by deflecting the electron beam using a magnetic field. The magnetic field comes from a set of deflection coils placed around the neck of the picture tube. The electron beam is emitted from the electron gun, with an energy of approximately 3.0 keV (before deflection). After deflection, the electrons are further accelerated. The dimensions of each part are shown in Figure. At maximum deflection, estimate the magnitude of the magnetic field B.
after_value: The scanning function of a television picture tube is achieved by deflecting the electron beam using a magnetic field. The magnetic field comes from a set of deflection coils placed around the neck of the picture tube. The electron beam is emitted from the electron gun, with an energy of approximately 3.0 keV (before deflection). After deflection, the electrons are further accelerated. The dimensions of each part are shown in Figure. At maximum deflection, estimate the magnitude of the magnetic field B.

after_value: A very long conducting rod has a hole inside it. The axis of the hole is parallel to the axis of the rod and separated by a distance $d$, as shown in Figure. A uniform current density $j$ flows through the conductor. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field $\boldsymbol{B}$ at a location on the axis of the hole far from the ends?

before_value: \( dj/2\epsilon_0c^2 \)

In a plastic film factory, a thin plastic wide belt moves at a speed $v$ between two adjacent rollers, as shown in Figure. During the manufacturing process, the thin film accumulates a uniform surface charge $\sigma$. Find the magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ in the same region.
after_value: In a plastic film factory, a thin plastic wide belt moves at a speed $v$ between two adjacent rollers, as shown in Figure. During the manufacturing process, the thin film accumulates a uniform surface charge $\sigma$. Find the magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ in the same region.

after_value: In the wire shown in the figure, the current is $I$. At the center of the semicircle, what is the magnetic field contribution $B$ from the semicircle of radius $r$?

Figure shows a uniform magnetic field B confined within a cylindrical volume. The magnitude of the magnetic field decreases at a constant rate of 100 gauss s⁻¹. Find the instantaneous acceleration of electrons located at P₁. Assume $a$ is 5.0 cm.
after_value: Figure shows a uniform magnetic field B confined within a cylindrical volume. The magnitude of the magnetic field decreases at a constant rate of 100 gauss s−1. Find the instantaneous acceleration of electrons located at P1. Assume $a$ is 5.0 cm.

after_value: A rigid wire is bent into a semicircle, and it rotates in a uniform magnetic field, as shown in Figure. If the internal resistance of the instrument M is $M_R$, and other resistances are negligible. Find the amplitude $I_0$ of the induced current.

after_value: As shown in the figure, a metal wire of mass $m$ slides frictionlessly on two rails. The rails are located in a vertical uniform magnetic field $B$. A constant current $I$ flows from generator $G$ into the rails and back along the other rail. Find the velocity of the wire as a function of time $v(t)$. (At $t=0$, the wire is at rest.)

before_value: \( \frac{IBd}{m}t \)

A small circular loop (1) carries a steady current $I$. Another circular loop (2), is coaxial with (1). The distance between the two loops is $ \gg a$. The planes of the two loops are parallel. As shown in Figure. Loop (2) now rotates about one of its diameters with angular velocity $\omega$. Loop (2) is broken (open circuit), so no current flows through it. Find the induced electromotive force $\epsilon$.
after_value: A small circular loop (1) carries a steady current $I$. Another circular loop (2) is coaxial with (1). The distance between the two loops is $\gg a$. The planes of the two loops are parallel. As shown in the figure, loop (2) now rotates about one of its diameters with angular velocity $\omega$. Loop (2) is broken (open circuit), so no current flows through it. Find the induced electromotive force $\epsilon$.

before_value: \( \frac{\pi a^4 I \omega}{2 \epsilon_0 c^2 R^3} \sin \omega t \)

after_value: A 1 W laser beam (of diameter 2 cm) falls normally on two circular holes each of diameter 0.05 cm, as shown in figure. after_value: A ring with mass $m$ is placed on a knife edge. The ring can oscillate in its own plane, forming a physical pendulum, as shown in the figure. Find the period $T$ for small oscillations of this ring.

The figure shows a Rayleigh interferometer used to measure the refractive index of air. Two identical glass tubes T₁ and T₂ are placed behind the double slits. Initially, tube T₁ is evacuated, and tube T₂ is filled with the air to be measured. After the experiment begins, air is slowly introduced into tube T₁ until the pressure in both tubes is equal. During the entire process, the intensity at point P was observed to change 98 times. The wavelength of the incident light is 589.3 nm, and the length of the tubes is 20 cm. Find the refractive index n of the air.
after_value: The figure shows a Rayleigh interferometer used to measure the refractive index of air. Two identical glass tubes T1 and T2 are placed behind the double slits. Initially, tube T1 is evacuated, and tube T2 is filled with the air to be measured. After the experiment begins, air is slowly introduced into tube T1 until the pressure in both tubes is equal. During the entire process, the intensity at point P was observed to change 98 times. The wavelength of the incident light is 589.3 nm, and the length of the tubes is 20 cm. Find the refractive index n of the air.

after_value: As shown in the figure, parallel monochromatic light with wavelength $\lambda$ is obliquely incident at a small angle $\alpha$ on a double slit. Find the position of the zero-order bright fringe.

before_value: As shown in the figure, two coherent point sources S₁ and S₂ produce interference fringes on a receiving screen 2 m away. before_value: A plate is attached to the lower end of a vertically suspended light spring (as shown in the figure). Find the equation of motion of the plate. (Take the equilibrium position after the object lands on the plate as the origin of the coordinate system, with downward displacement being positive.)
after_value: A plate is attached to the lower end of a vertically suspended light spring (as shown in the figure). An object of mass m falls freely from a height above the plate and lands on the plate without rebounding. Taking the instant the object lands on the plate as the origin of time. before_value: \( \frac{mg}{k} \sqrt{1 + \frac{2kh}{(M+m)g}} \cos \left[ \sqrt{\frac{k}{M+m}} t + \left( \arctan \sqrt{\frac{2kh}{(M+m)g}} + \pi \right) \right] \)

after_value: A pendulum is made of a light rod and a uniform circular disk. The disk is rigidly connected to the rod as shown in the figure. When the pendulum undergoes small oscillations, find the period.

before_value: \( 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{r^2 + 2l^2}{2gl}} \)

after_value: A pendulum is made of a light rod and a uniform circular disk. The disk is mounted on a smooth pivot at the end of the rod, allowing it to rotate freely relative to it, as shown in the figure. When the pendulum undergoes small oscillations, find the period.

As shown in the figure, one end of a light spring is fixed, and the other end is attached to a light string. The light string passes over a pulley and is connected to an object. The string does not slip on the pulley, allowing the object to oscillate freely up and down. The moment of inertia of the pulley is $J$. Find the period of oscillation of the object.
after_value: As shown in the figure, one end of a light spring is fixed, and the other end is attached to a light string. The light string passes over a pulley and is connected to an object. The string does not slip on the pulley, allowing the object to oscillate freely up and down. The moment of inertia of the pulley is $J$. Find the period of oscillation of the object.

before_value: \( 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m + \frac{J}{R^2}}{k}} \)

The distance between the wave source S on the ground and the high-frequency wave detector D is $d$. The wave directly emitted from S and the wave emitted from S and reflected by a horizontal layer interfere constructively at D. The angle of reflection and the angle of incidence with the horizontal layer are the same. When the horizontal layer is gradually raised, no signal is detected at D. Neglect atmospheric absorption. Find the wavelength of the wave emitted by the wave source S.
after_value: The distance between the wave source S on the ground and the high-frequency wave detector D is d. The wave directly emitted from S and the wave emitted from S and reflected by a horizontal layer interfere constructively at D. The angle of reflection and the angle of incidence with the horizontal layer are the same. When the horizontal layer is gradually raised, no signal is detected at D. Neglect atmospheric absorption. Find the wavelength of the wave emitted by the wave source S.

before_value: \( 2 \left[\sqrt{4(H+h)^2 + d^2} - \sqrt{4H^2 + d^2}\right] \)

As shown in the figure, three simple harmonic waves with the same frequency and the same oscillation direction (perpendicular to the plane of the paper) meet at point P during propagation. The oscillation equations for the three simple harmonic waves alone at S1, S2, and S3 are respectively:$y_1 = A \cos(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{2})$ $y_2 = A \cos(\omega t)$ $y_3 = A \cos(\omega t - \frac{\pi}{2})$ If $S_2P = 4\lambda$, $S_1P = S_3P = 5\lambda$ (where $\lambda$ is the wavelength), find the equation for the combined oscillation at point P (Assume the amplitude of each wave does not change during propagation).
after_value: As shown in the figure, three simple harmonic waves with the same frequency and the same oscillation direction (perpendicular to the plane of the paper) meet at point P during propagation. The oscillation equations for the three simple harmonic waves alone at S1, S2, and S3 are respectively: $y_1 = A \cos(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{2})$, $y_2 = A \cos(\omega t)$, $y_3 = A \cos(\omega t - \frac{\pi}{2})$. If $S_2P = 4\lambda$, $S_1P = S_3P = 5\lambda$ (where $\lambda$ is the wavelength), find the equation for the combined oscillation at point P. Assume the amplitude of each wave does not change during propagation.

before_value: \( A \cos\omega t \)

before_value: When manufacturing semiconductor devices, it is often necessary to accurately measure the thickness of the silicon dioxide ($\text{SiO}_2$) thin film on a silicon wafer. It is known that the refractive index of Si is 3.42, and the refractive index of $\text{SiO}_2$ is 1.5. Find the thickness of the $\text{SiO}_2$ thin film.

When illuminated perpendicularly with a helium-neon laser ($\lambda = 632.8 \text{ nm}$), 8 dark fringes are observed in the etched region in the reflected light. Find the thickness of the $\text{SiO}_2$ thin film.

before_value: A Mach-Zehnder interferometer is commonly used to measure the refractive index of gases under various temperatures and pressures. The optical path of the interferometer is shown in the figure. S is the light source, L is the converging lens, G₁ and G₂ are two parallel glass plates of equal thickness, and T₁ and T₂ are two glass tubes of equal length.When performing the measurement, first evacuate T₁ and T₂, and then slowly introduce the gas to be measured into one tube. By observing the change in interference fringes at point E, the refractive index of the gas to be measured can be determined. For example, in a measurement of a certain gas, the gas is slowly introduced into tube T₂, and when the gas reaches standard conditions, a total of 98 interference fringes are observed to move at point E. The wavelength of the yellow light used is 589.3 nm (in vacuum), and l = 20 cm. Find the refractive index of this gas under standard conditions.
after_value: A Mach-Zehnder interferometer is commonly used to measure the refractive index of gases under various temperatures and pressures. The optical path of the interferometer is shown in the figure. When performing the measurement, first evacuate T1 and T2, and then slowly introduce the gas to be measured into one tube. By observing the change in interference fringes at point E, the refractive index of the gas to be measured can be determined. For example, in a measurement of a certain gas, the gas is slowly introduced into tube T2, and when the gas reaches standard conditions, a total of 98 interference fringes are observed to move at point E. The wavelength of the yellow light used is 589.3 nm (in vacuum), and l = 20 cm. Find the refractive index of this gas under standard conditions.

The structure and working principle of a Rayleigh interferometer are as follows (as shown in Figure): A sodium vapor lamp is used as the light source, placed in front of lens $L_1$. The changes in interference fringes are observed on the focal plane of lens $L_2$. Two identical glass tubes, $T_1$ and $T_2$, are placed between the two lenses. At the beginning of the experiment, $T_2$ is filled with air, and $T_1$ is evacuated to a vacuum. At this time, the interference fringes are observed. Then, air is gradually introduced into tube $T_1$ until the air pressure in $T_1$ and $T_2$ is the same. The number of fringes that have shifted during this process is recorded. Given that the wavelength of light is 589.3 nm, the tube length is 20 cm, and 98 fringes have shifted, find the refractive index of air.

What should the distance \(l\) between the forces be if they are to provide a net torque of \(6.40\,\text{N}\cdot\text{m}\) about the left end of the rod?

before_value: You are doing exercises on a Nautilus machine in a gym to strengthen your deltoid (shoulder) muscles. Your arms are raised vertically and can pivot around the shoulder joint. before_value: 20kg

Sir Lancelot rides slowly out of the castle at Camelot and onto the drawbridge that passes over the moat. Unbeknownst to him, his enemies have partially severed the vertical cable holding up the front end of the bridge so that it will break under a tension of \( 5.80 \times 10^3\, \text{N} \). The bridge has mass \( 200\, \text{kg} \) and its center of gravity is at its center. Lancelot, his lance, his armor, and his horse together have a combined mass of \( 600\, \text{kg} \). How far from the castle end of the bridge will the center of gravity of the horse plus rider be when the cable breaks?
after_value: Sir Lancelot rides slowly out of the castle at Camelot and onto the drawbridge that passes over the moat. before_value: Mountaineers often use a rope to lower themselves down the face of a cliff (this is called \textit{rappelling}). They do this with their body nearly horizontal and their feet pushing against the cliff. Suppose that an \( 82.0\, \text{kg} \) climber, who is \( 1.90\, \text{m} \) tall and has a center of gravity \( 1.1\, \text{m} \) from his feet, rappels down a vertical cliff with his body raised \( 35.0^\circ \) above the horizontal. He holds the rope \( 1.40\, \text{m} \) from his feet, and it makes a \( 25.0^\circ \) angle with the cliff face. What minimum coefficient of static friction is needed to prevent the climber’s feet from slipping on the cliff face if he has one foot at a time against the cliff?
after_value: Mountaineers often use a rope to lower themselves down the face of a cliff (this is called \textit{rappelling}). Suppose that an \(82.0\, \text{kg}\) climber, who is \(1.90\, \text{m}\) tall and has a center of gravity \(1.1\, \text{m}\) from his feet, rappels down a vertical cliff with his body raised \(35.0^\circ\) above the horizontal. He holds the rope \(1.40\, \text{m}\) from his feet, and it makes a \(25.0^\circ\) angle with the cliff face. What minimum coefficient of static friction is needed to prevent the climber’s feet from slipping on the cliff face if he has one foot at a time against the cliff?

before_value: A uniform, \( 255\, \text{N} \) rod that is \( 2.00\, \text{m} \) long carries a weight at its right end and an unknown weight \( W \) toward the left end. before_value: A therapist tells a \( 74\, \text{kg} \) patient with a broken leg that he must have his leg in a cast suspended horizontally. For minimum discomfort, the leg should be supported by a vertical strap attached at the center of mass of the leg--cast system. To comply with these instructions, the patient consults a table of typical mass distributions and finds that both upper legs (thighs) together typically account for \( 21.5\% \) of body weight and the center of mass of each thigh is \( 18.0\, \text{cm} \) from the hip joint. The patient also reads that the two lower legs (including the feet) are \( 14.0\% \) of body weight, with a center of mass \( 69.0\, \text{cm} \) from the hip joint. The cast has a mass of \( 5.50\, \text{kg} \), and its center of mass is \( 78.0\, \text{cm} \) from the hip joint. How far from the hip joint should the supporting strap be attached to the cast?
after_value: A therapist tells a \(74\,\text{kg}\) patient with a broken leg that he must have his leg in a cast suspended horizontally. The patient also reads that the two lower legs (including the feet) are \(14.0\%\) of body weight, with a center of mass \(69.0\,\text{cm}\) from the hip joint. The cast has a mass of \(5.50\,\text{kg}\), and its center of mass is \(78.0\,\text{cm}\) from the hip joint. How far from the hip joint should the supporting strap be attached to the cast?

before_value: A \( 72.0\, \text{kg} \) weightlifter doing arm raises holds a weight. Her arm pivots around the elbow joint, starting below the horizontal. Biometric measurements have shown that, together, the forearms and the hands account for \( 6.00\% \) of a person’s weight. Since the upper arm is held vertically, the biceps muscle always acts vertically and is attached to the bones of the forearm \( 5.50\, \text{cm} \) from the elbow joint. The center of mass of this person’s forearm–hand combination is \( 16.0\, \text{cm} \) from the elbow joint, along the bones of the forearm, and she holds the weight. Find the magnitude of the force that the elbow joint exerts on the forearm.
after_value: A \(72.0\,\text{kg}\) weightlifter doing arm raises holds a weight. Her arm pivots around the elbow joint, starting below the horizontal. before_value: As part of an exercise program, a \( 75\, \text{kg} \) person does toe raises in which he raises his entire body weight on the ball of one foot. The Achilles tendon pulls straight upward on the heel bone of his foot. This tendon is \( 25\, \text{cm} \) long and has a cross-sectional area of \( 78\, \text{mm}^2 \) and a Young’s modulus of \( 1470\, \text{MPa} \). By how many millimeters does the exercise stretch his Achilles tendon?
after_value: As part of an exercise program, a \( 75\, \text{kg} \) person does toe raises in which he raises his entire body weight on the ball of one foot. before_value: 4.4mm

before_value: The yoga exercise ``Downward-Facing Dog'' requires stretching your hands straight out above your head and bending down to lean against the floor. This exercise is performed by a \( 750\, \text{N} \) person as shown in figure. When he bends his body at the hip, his legs, trunk, head, and arms have the dimensions indicated. Furthermore, his legs and feet weigh a total of \( 277\, \text{N} \), and their center of mass is \( 41\, \text{cm} \) from his hip, measured along his legs. The person’s trunk, head, and arms weigh \( 473\, \text{N} \), and their center of gravity is \( 65\, \text{cm} \) from his hip, measured along the upper body. Find the friction force on each foot and on each hand, assuming that it is the same on both feet and on both hands
